title: Childhood Trauma Echoes Through Romantic Relationships
gist: A University of Georgia study of over 200 couples finds that greater childhood trauma predicts more adult loneliness, depression, and anxiety, and spills into romantic relationships as difficulty communicating, showing affection, and managing conflict.

insights.claim: In a University of Georgia study of more than 200 opposite-sex couples, those who experienced more childhood trauma, including abuse, neglect, divorce, or parental death, were more likely to experience adult loneliness, depression, and anxiety.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
entities: Journal of Social and Personal Relationships
insights.so_what: Links measurable childhood adversity to adult mental-health outcomes.

insights.claim: Childhood trauma also degraded participants' romantic lives, leaving those with rougher childhoods struggling to communicate, show affection, and manage conflict with partners.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Extends adverse-childhood-experience effects into partner relationships.
insights.locator: The researchers say these negative effects

top_entities: Journal of Social and Personal Relationships
